senses.definition: An early or primitive type of mouth in some ancient or simple animals, especially in discussions of their evolution.
examples: The fossil showed an archaeostoma that opened on the underside of the body.
Researchers compared the archaeostoma of several primitive marine species.

senses.definition: A proposed or historical taxonomic group of very primitive animals characterized by a simple or ancient type of mouth.
examples: Some early zoologists placed these organisms in the Archaeostomata.
The class Archaeostomata is no longer widely used in modern taxonomy.

senses.definition: Having an early or primitive type of mouth, as described for some ancient or simple animals.
examples: The larvae show an archaeostomatous arrangement of the digestive opening.
These archaeostomatous features suggest a very old evolutionary lineage.

senses.definition: A genus of large, pig-like prehistoric mammals known from fossils in North America and Europe.
examples: The archaeotherium skull displayed impressive tusks and a strong jaw.
Paleontologists believe archaeotherium was an omnivorous scavenger.

senses.definition: The study of animal remains from archaeological sites to understand past human life and environments.
examples: Archaeozoology can reveal what people ate thousands of years ago.
She decided to specialize in archaeozoology during her graduate studies.

senses.definition: In old science and philosophy, a supposed vital force or spirit believed to control natural processes in the body.
examples: The physician wrote about the archaeus as the source of life and health.
According to this theory, disease was caused by a disturbance in the archaeus.

senses.definition: Plural of archaezoology, referring to multiple bodies or traditions of study dealing with animal remains from archaeological sites.
examples: Different national archaezoologies have developed their own methods.
The book compared European and American archaezoologies.

senses.definition: Alternative spelling of archaeozoology, the study of animal remains from archaeological sites.
examples: She is writing her thesis in archaezoology.
Archaezoology has transformed our understanding of ancient diets.

senses.definition: The main person who stirs up trouble, protest, or opposition, especially in a political or social group.
examples: He was seen as the archagitator behind the student protests.
The government blamed an external archagitator for the unrest.

senses.definition: Plural of archon, used in some discussions of classical Greek history to refer to chief magistrates of ancient Athens and other cities.
examples: The archai were responsible for important religious and legal duties.
In some periods, the archai held office for only one year.

senses.definition: Old and no longer used in modern language or writing.
examples: Thou art is an archaic way of saying you are.
The poem uses several archaic words and spellings.

senses.definition: Very old-fashioned and no longer suitable or useful today.
examples: Some people see marriage as an archaic institution.
The company still relies on an archaic computer system.

senses.definition: Relating to or coming from a very early and ancient period in history or in the development of something.
examples: The museum houses archaic Greek sculptures.
Archaic tools were found at the bottom of the cave.

senses.definition: Very old-fashioned or belonging to a much earlier time, used especially about language or style.
examples: The author deliberately chose an archaical style of writing.
These archaical turns of phrase can be hard for modern readers.

senses.definition: In an old-fashioned way or using language or style from a much earlier time.
examples: The characters speak archaically to match the historical setting.
Some legal documents are still written archaically.

senses.definition: The use of very old or old-fashioned words or styles in speech or writing.
examples: The poem is full of deliberate archaicism.
Critics complained about the novel's heavy archaicism.

senses.definition: Plural of archaicism, referring to separate cases of using very old or old-fashioned language or style.
examples: The text contains several archaicisms that confuse students.
These archaicisms were added to imitate medieval writing.

senses.definition: The quality of being very old-fashioned or belonging to a much earlier time.
examples: The archaicness of the law makes it hard to apply today.
Some readers enjoy the archaicness of his prose.

senses.definition: To make something, especially language or style, seem older or from a much earlier time.
examples: The playwright chose to archaise the dialogue to fit the setting.
Writers sometimes archaise their style for artistic effect.

senses.definition: Made to sound or look old-fashioned or from a much earlier time, especially in language or style.
examples: The poem is written in an archaised version of English.
An archaised script was used in the film's titles.

senses.definition: A person who likes to use very old-fashioned language or style, or who makes things look deliberately old.
examples: As an archaiser, he filled his poems with old words.
Critics saw him as an archaiser rather than an innovator.

senses.definition: People who like to use or create very old-fashioned language or styles.
examples: Some writers are archaisers who resist modern expressions.
The group of archaisers worked to revive early forms of the language.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of archaise, meaning to make language or style look or sound old-fashioned.
examples: The author archaises the narration in the final chapter.
He deliberately archaises his prose in historical novels.

senses.definition: Showing a deliberate use of old-fashioned language or style.
examples: The play uses archaising dialogue to create a medieval atmosphere.
An archaising tone runs through the entire novel.

senses.definition: Present participle of archaise, meaning making something look or sound old-fashioned.
examples: By archaising the text, the editor hoped to match the original style.
She is archaising the translation to give it a classical feel.

senses.definition: A word, expression, or style that was used in the past but is no longer common today.
examples: The poem contains several archaisms that may puzzle modern readers.
Using archaism can give a text a historical flavor.

senses.definition: The quality of being old-fashioned or out of date.
examples: Some see the royal ceremony as pure archaism.
The archaism of these customs makes them attractive to tourists.

senses.definition: Plural of archaism, referring to several old or old-fashioned words, expressions, or styles.
examples: The text is full of archaisms and unusual spellings.
Teachers often explain archaisms when reading Shakespeare with students.

senses.definition: A person who likes or supports the use of old-fashioned words, styles, or customs.
examples: As an archaist, he resisted modern spelling reforms.
The painter was an archaist who copied medieval techniques.

senses.definition: Showing a strong preference for old-fashioned styles, especially in art, language, or customs.
examples: The building has an archaistic design inspired by ancient temples.
Her archaistic language gives the novel a historical flavor.

senses.definition: People who favor or use old-fashioned language, styles, or customs.
examples: The group of archaists pushed for the return of traditional forms.
Some critics call them archaists rather than innovators.

senses.definition: To make something, especially language or style, deliberately old-fashioned.
examples: The translator chose to archaize several key passages.
Writers sometimes archaize to give their work a classical tone.

senses.definition: Made to appear or sound old, especially in language, design, or style.
examples: The text is an archaized version of the original story.
They live in an archaized village built for tourists.

senses.definition: Someone who deliberately makes language, art, or customs look old-fashioned.
examples: The poet is a conscious archaizer of English.
As an archaizer, she prefers medieval forms and patterns.

senses.definition: People who deliberately make language, art, or customs look old-fashioned.
examples: The archaizers in the movement opposed any modern influences.
Language archaizers often focus on spelling and vocabulary.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of archaize, meaning makes something deliberately old-fashioned.
examples: He archaizes his dialogue to match the historical period.
The film archaizes the setting to create a sense of distance.

senses.definition: Showing deliberate imitation of old or ancient styles in language, art, or design.
examples: The archaizing sculptures copied ancient Greek models.
An archaizing tone runs through his early poetry.

senses.definition: Present participle of archaize, meaning making something look or sound old-fashioned on purpose.
examples: By archaizing the dialogue, the director aimed for authenticity.
He is archaizing the text to reflect its historical origins.

senses.definition: An angel of very high rank in some religions.
examples: The Archangel Gabriel appears in several religious stories.
In the painting, an archangel stands above the crowd with a sword.

senses.definition: Relating to an archangel or having the qualities of an archangel.
examples: The choir sang with an almost archangelic purity.
He is depicted with archangelic wings and armor.

senses.definition: A plant of the angelica family, sometimes used in medicine or as a flavoring.
examples: The herbalist grew archangelica in the garden.
Dried archangelica roots are used in traditional remedies.

senses.definition: Relating to an archangel, especially in an old or literary style.
examples: The poet used archangelical imagery to describe the hero.
An archangelical host was painted on the ceiling.

senses.definition: Plural of archangel, referring to angels of very high rank in some religions.
examples: In the story, three archangels visit the prophet.
The ceiling is decorated with archangels and saints.

senses.definition: The state or office of being an archangel, especially in theological or literary writing.
examples: His archangelship is described in ancient texts.
The poem celebrates Michael's archangelship.

senses.definition: The main or most important opponent or enemy in a situation or story.
examples: The novel's archantagonist is a corrupt businessman.
He became the archantagonist of the reform movement.

senses.definition: Relating to very early or primitive human-like beings.
examples: The site contains archanthropine remains.
These archanthropine features suggest an early stage in human evolution.

senses.definition: A chief or leading expert in old objects, records, or traditions.
examples: He was considered the archantiquary of the kingdom.
As archantiquary, she oversaw the royal collections.

senses.definition: A person seen as the main or worst example of someone who has rejected their religion or beliefs.
examples: He was branded the archapostate by his former church.
The story portrays the king as an archapostate who betrayed his faith.

senses.definition: A leading or principal apostle, or a chief champion of a belief or cause.
examples: He was hailed as the archapostle of non-violence.
In legend, Peter appears as an archapostle of the early church.

senses.definition: A chief or very important architect; sometimes used figuratively for someone who designs or plans something major.
examples: He is widely regarded as the archarchitect of the city's new skyline.
As archarchitect of the reforms, she shaped the entire program.

senses.definition: In some Christian monastic traditions, a novice or beginner monk.
examples: The archarios spent his first year in quiet study and prayer.
As an archarios, he was assigned simple tasks around the monastery.

senses.definition: A leading or outstanding artist; sometimes used approvingly or ironically.
examples: He was seen as the archartist of his generation.
The archartist behind the project remained unknown.

senses.definition: An old term for a chief bench or main seat, especially a judge's high seat in a court.
examples: He took his place on the archbanc to open the session.
The ornate archbanc dominated the small courtroom.

senses.definition: Plural of archbanc, referring to chief benches or main seats, especially for judges.
examples: The hall was lined with archbancs for the senior judges.
Old drawings show several archbancs facing the crowd.

senses.definition: A curved or arched band, especially as part of a piece of clothing or an architectural detail.
examples: The dress was finished with a delicate archband around the neckline.
An archband of stone framed the doorway.
